Mangroves can filter up to 90% of the salt found in seawater before it ever reaches their leaves.
A single mangrove tree can host over 100 species of fish, crab, and shellfish among its roots.
Mangrove forests store carbon up to four times more efficiently per hectare than tropical rainforests.
The tangled prop roots of mangroves slow down waves, protecting coastal barangays like ours from storm surge.
Some mangrove seeds, called propagules, can float in seawater for over a year before taking root.
Mangrove leaves can excrete excess salt directly through their surface — you can sometimes see the salt crystals!
The Philippines is home to about half of the world's 70+ known mangrove species.
